findings.description: parse() reads columns by hardcoded positional indices (row[0..3]) instead of the computed name->index map, so any header column order other than id,name,amount,email assigns wrong values or crashes.
The 'index' dict mapping column names to positions is built but never used, dead code that masks the order-dependence bug above.
A data row with fewer than 4 fields raises an uncaught IndexError, aborting the entire parse on a single malformed/short/blank row.
int(row[0]) and float(row[2]) raise uncaught ValueError on non-numeric data, giving no row context and crashing the whole ingest.
open(path).readlines() never closes the file handle (ResourceWarning/leak) and loads the entire file into memory.
count_rows returns -1 for an empty file and miscounts CSVs containing quoted embedded newlines or no trailing newline, since it counts raw text lines instead of using csv.reader.
stream_valid is documented/named as streaming but calls self.parse(raw) which fully materializes all records first, so it provides no streaming/memory benefit.
Exported CSV does not neutralize leading =,+,-,@ in name/email fields, enabling CSV/formula injection when the 'cleaned' file is opened in a spreadsheet.
open() calls for reading and writing specify no encoding, relying on the platform default which can corrupt or fail on non-ASCII (UTF-8) names/emails.
parse() does list(reader) loading the whole CSV into memory at once, with no chunked/streaming path for large uploads.
os.path.basename mitigates typical path traversal but an empty or directory-like filename yields an empty basename, causing a write to the directory path (IsADirectoryError) with no validation.
Constructor performs a filesystem side effect (os.makedirs) and can raise PermissionError for /var/data/clean simply when instantiating the object.
EXPORT_DIR is a hardcoded absolute path, reducing portability and testability beyond the constructor override.
Duplicate header column names silently collapse to the last index in the comprehension with no warning (latent if the index map were ever used).
summarize() sums floats directly, so accumulated floating-point error can make total/avg slightly imprecise for monetary amounts.
